nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily vegetated ditches, canals, and swampy areas among Nipa palm (Nypa fruticans) stands in the Mahachai District of Samut Sakhon Province; water is often turbid, slow-moving, and subject to seasonal brackish influence from tidal incursion
nativeRange.biotope: Nipa palm swamp / brackish-influenced lowland canal

decorPreferences: dense floating plants (frogbit, water lettuce, Nipa palm leaf litter substitute)
broad-leaved plants as resting sites (Anubias, Amazon sword)
driftwood and dried botanicals for tannins
caves or coconut shells for bubble-nest anchoring
minimal water flow — near-still surface essential for bubble-nest integrity

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild, consuming small insects, larvae, zooplankton, and other invertebrates. In captivity, accepts high-quality micro-pellets and frozen/live foods: bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, mosquito larvae, and blackworms. Live or frozen foods are strongly preferred and help maintain condition and coloration. Dried flake alone is generally insufficient as a staple.

goodWith: small snails (Nerite, Ramshorn) in species tanks
small, fast-moving, non-nipping tankmates in very large tanks with visual breaks (otocinclus, small rasboras in a brackish-tolerant setup)
female Mahachai Bettas — only with ample space and dense cover, monitored closely
avoidWith: other male Betta species or B. mahachaiensis males (severe aggression)
slow-moving or long-finned fish that may be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo gallery for Betta mahachaiensis (species ID 72644); check for available CC-licensed images of males in breeding coloration.
Wikimedia Commons category for B. mahachaiensis; verify availability and license before use.

summary: The Mahachai Betta is a wild Betta described in 2012 from Nipa palm swamps in Samut Sakhon, Thailand. Unlike most wild Bettas it tolerates mild brackish conditions and builds bubble nests. Habitat destruction makes wild populations vulnerable; tank-bred stock is strongly preferred.
careNotes:
Betta mahachaiensis was formally described in 2012 by Kowasupat et al. and remains one of the more recently documented wild Betta species. Its natural habitat — shallow, tannin-rich canals and ditches among Nipa palm stands near the coast of Samut Sakhon Province — is subject to seasonal tidal influence, making this species uniquely euryhaline among freshwater Bettas. While pure freshwater is tolerated, adding a small amount of marine salt to raise salinity to SG 1.001–1.003 can improve health and replicate natural conditions; harder alkaline water is also better tolerated than by most Betta species.

A single male should be kept alone or with carefully selected, non-aggressive tankmates in a well-planted tank of at least 40 L. The labyrinth organ requires access to warm, humid air above the water surface; keep the tank covered with a tight-fitting lid to maintain air temperature close to water temperature. Near-still surface water is essential — strong filtration current will destroy bubble nests and stress the fish. A sponge filter is recommended.

Wild-caught specimens may be available but tank-bred stock is strongly preferred due to conservation concerns: the Mahachai Betta’s highly restricted range (essentially a single Thai district) is under intense pressure from urban and industrial development in the Greater Bangkok area. Several hobbyist conservation breeding programs exist. This species should not be confused with Betta splendens (the common Siamese Fighting Fish), to which it is closely related but morphologically and ecologically distinct.
breedingNotes: B. mahachaiensis is a bubble-nest builder, unlike the mouthbrooding members of the Betta complex. The male constructs a foam nest at the water surface, often anchored among floating plants or under a broad leaf. Condition both fish with live or frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before introducing the female. The female may be placed in a transparent divider to allow visual acclimation before full introduction. Spawning embrace is typical of anabantoids; the male wraps around the female and fertilizes eggs as they are released. The male collects and places eggs in the bubble nest and should be left to guard them; the female should be removed after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; the male tends the nest until fry are free-swimming (around day 3–4). Remove the male once fry are free-swimming. First foods: infusoria, vinegar eels, or commercial fry food, progressing to micro worms and baby brine shrimp. Breeding difficulty is moderate due to the need for stable water quality, correct sexing, and careful introduction.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta mahachaiensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided description date (2012, Kowasupat et al.), native range (Samut Sakhon, Thailand), habitat (Nipa palm swamps, brackish-influenced), water parameters, adult size, diet, compatibility, breeding behavior (bubble-nester), and conservation notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Betta mahachaiensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), adult size (to ~7 cm), native country (Thailand), and synonymy. Confirmed species described 2012 by Kowasupat, Panijpan, Ruenwongsa, and Sriwattanarothai.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Wild Betta Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for general wild Betta husbandry guidance: tank size, near-still water, diet, and labyrinth organ requirements applicable to B. mahachaiensis. No species-specific page for B. mahachaiensis exists on Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Betta mahachaiensis species profile
additional.notes: Referenced for conservation status discussion (habitat loss from urbanization in Samut Sakhon), brackish tolerance confirmation, and hobbyist captive-breeding programs. Corroborated bubble-nesting behavior.

additional.name: Kowasupat et al. 2012 — Original species description (Zootaxa 3522)
additional.notes: Original scientific description of Betta mahachaiensis. Provided type locality (Mahachai District, Samut Sakhon Province, Thailand), morphological data, and ecological notes on Nipa palm swamp habitat.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder. Male constructs foam nest at surface; female removed after spawning, male removed when fry are free-swimming (~day 3–4). First foods: infusoria or commercial fry powder, then micro worms and baby brine shrimp.
